title: Fix class strict execution contexts
description: staging/sm/class/strictExecution.js passes strict wrapper but fails non-strict with Test262Error. The file checks that class constructors, computed property names, and heritage expressions execute in strict mode even when enclosing script code is sloppy.
acceptance: Reproduce strictExecution.js; identify which class evaluation context is still sloppy; add focused regression coverage; verify the filtered Test262 file and relevant parser/compiler/class tests; update notes and move to in_review.
notes: Implemented strict assignment lowering for class computed-name and heritage expression evaluation in sloppy outer code. Reproduced staging/sm/class/strictExecution.js before as 0/1 files failed, with only the non-strict variant failing Test262Error in /tmp/lyng-js-class-strict-execution-before.md. Root cause: class constructor bodies were already strict, but computed class element names were evaluated in the enclosing sloppy frame, so assignment to a non-extensible object silently failed instead of throwing TypeError. Fix adds StrictAssignNamedProperty and StrictAssignKeyedProperty bytecode opcodes, VM dispatch checks with a strict override, and a compiler force_strict_assignment mode used while lowering class heritage/computed key expressions. Added regression phase6_class_computed_names_use_strict_assignment_semantics. Verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-tests phase6_class_computed_names_use_strict_assignment_semantics -- --nocapture passed; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/strictExecution.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-strict-execution-after.md -j 1 passed 1/1 files, 2/2 variants; cargo test -p lyng-js-bytecode passed 28 unit tests and doc tests; cargo test -p lyng-js-compiler class -- --nocapture passed 7 tests; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests classes -- --nocapture passed 66 tests; cargo fmt --all ran.

title: Fix global object proxy receiver binding semantics
description: staging/sm/Proxy/global-receiver.js fails because bare global identifier get/set through a proxy on the global prototype throws ReferenceError instead of using the global object as receiver.
acceptance: Focused Test262 filter staging/sm/Proxy/global-receiver.js passes both variants; add a Lyng JS regression for global prototype proxy receiver get/set semantics.

title: Run SpiderMonkey non262 expression harness tests non-strict only
description: staging/sm/expressions/destructuring-array-default-*.js fail only in strict variants. Local reduction shows the strict wrapper reaches sloppy-only default initializer expressions such as var [x=[a='foo']=[]] = [], which correctly throw ReferenceError in strict mode. The sm/non262-expressions-shell.js helper should not be outer strict-wrapped by the runner.
acceptance: Tests including sm/non262-expressions-shell.js select only the non-strict variant; focused destructuring-array-default staging filter passes without hiding non-strict failures; add metadata unit coverage for the variant policy.

comments.author: 5341456+Feryla@users.noreply.github.com
comments.text: Fixed as harness variant policy, not engine semantics. Root cause: sm/non262-expressions-shell.js generates expressions like var [x=[a='foo']=[]] = [], which correctly throw ReferenceError under an outer strict wrapper because a is an unresolvable assignment target. The SpiderMonkey expression stress helper should run non-strict only, like the existing non262 strict-shell policy. Added metadata unit coverage for sm/non262-expressions-shell.js. Verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-test262 spidermonkey_expression_shell_tests_are_non_strict_only -- --nocapture (red before fix, green after); cargo test -p lyng-js-test262 --lib spidermonkey_expression_shell_tests_are_non_strict_only -- --nocapture; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/expressions/destructuring-array-default- --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-destructuring-array-default-after.md -j 1 (6/6 files, 6/6 variants passed); cargo fmt --all; git diff --check.

comments.author: 5341456+Feryla@users.noreply.github.com
comments.text: Re-verified on the current tree before review. Metadata policy still selects only NonStrict for sm/non262-expressions-shell.js. Verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-test262 spidermonkey_expression_shell_tests_are_non_strict_only -- --nocapture; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/expressions/destructuring-array-default- --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-destructuring-array-default-after-current.md -j 1 (6/6 files, 6/6 variants passed); git diff --check.

title: Keep class name bindings live for field initializers
description: staging/sm/class/fields-instance-class-name-binding.js and fields-static-class-name-binding.js fail with MissingEnvironment(EnvironmentRef(4)). Class field initializer closures should resolve the class inner name binding even after outer bindings are rebound.
acceptance: Focused Test262 filters for fields-instance-class-name-binding.js and fields-static-class-name-binding.js pass both variants; add Lyng JS class regression coverage for instance and static field initializer name binding closure behavior.

comments.author: 5341456+Feryla@users.noreply.github.com
comments.text: Implemented class field initializer environment-depth accounting for closures that capture the class inner-name binding. Static field arrows now count the synthetic lexical-this arrow context; instance field initializer functions also count the constructor initializer environment. Added compiler coverage for static depth=1 and instance depth=2 class-name captures, plus Lyng JS execution coverage for instance/static declarations and named class expressions after rebinding the outer class variable. Verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-compiler compile_script_counts_class_field_arrow_context_in_capture_depths -- --nocapture; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests phase6_class_field_initializers_capture_inner_class_name_binding -- --nocapture; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/fields-instance-class-name-binding.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-name-binding-instance-after.md -j 1; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/fields-static-class-name-binding.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-name-binding-static-after.md -j 1; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests classes -- --nocapture; cargo test -p lyng-js-compiler; cargo fmt --all; git diff --check.

title: Fix eval class field name binding initializers
description: staging/sm/class/fields-instance-class-name-binding-eval.js fails both variants with Test262Error and fields-static-class-name-binding-eval.js fails both variants with ReferenceError. Eval executed from class field initializers should resolve the class inner-name binding consistently with non-eval initializer closures.
acceptance: Focused Test262 filters for fields-instance-class-name-binding-eval.js and fields-static-class-name-binding-eval.js pass both variants; add Lyng JS regression coverage for direct eval in instance and static field initializers after rebinding the outer class variable.

comments.author: 5341456+Feryla@users.noreply.github.com
comments.text: Fixed class field direct-eval name binding handling. Field initializer lowering now pushes the active class-body lexical scope into direct-eval site metadata, child functions created inside field initializers inherit those eval scopes, and explicit constructor instance-field initialization establishes the active class body. Added compiler metadata coverage plus execution coverage for direct eval and arrow eval in instance/static field initializers, including anonymous static class expression TDZ behavior. Verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-compiler; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests classes -- --nocapture; focused Test262 filters staging/sm/class/fields-instance-class-name-binding-eval.js and fields-static-class-name-binding-eval.js both pass 2/2 variants; cargo fmt --all; git diff --check.

title: Allow direct eval of new.target and super in class contexts
description: Fresh staging run /tmp/lyng-js-staging-current.md still fails SpiderMonkey class direct-eval cases with SyntaxError: staging/sm/class/newTargetEval.js, derivedConstructorArrowEvalSuperCall.js, derivedConstructorArrowEvalNestedSuperCall.js, and superPropEvalInsideArrow.js. Direct eval needs to preserve class constructor/method/arrow context allowances for new.target and super rather than rejecting them during dynamic eval parse/sema filtering.
acceptance: Focused Test262 filters for staging/sm/class/newTargetEval.js, staging/sm/class/derivedConstructorArrowEvalSuperCall.js, staging/sm/class/derivedConstructorArrowEvalNestedSuperCall.js, and staging/sm/class/superPropEvalInsideArrow.js pass all runnable variants; add targeted Lyng JS regression coverage for direct eval of new.target and super from class constructor/method arrow contexts.

comments.author: 5341456+Feryla@users.noreply.github.com
comments.text: Partial implementation landed for direct-eval caller context propagation. Added direct-eval site flags for new.target/super allowances, carried those through direct-eval analysis and nested direct eval sites, preserved new.target on eval entry contexts, and made eval super-property contexts use lexical-this eval environments so outer method state is used. Added regression coverage for nested direct eval of new.target through ordinary functions/arrows while keeping top-level arrow eval invalid. Verification so far: cargo test -p lyng-js-tests direct_eval_propagates_new_target_context_to_nested_eval -- --nocapture; focused Test262 staging/sm/class/newTargetEval.js and staging/sm/class/superPropEvalInsideArrow.js both pass 2/2 variants; cargo fmt --all; git diff --check. Still in progress: derivedConstructorArrowEvalSuperCall.js now fails ReferenceError, and derivedConstructorArrowEvalNestedSuperCall.js still fails SyntaxError/derived super-call context; this appears tied to the broader arrow super() initialization path rather than the direct eval new.target/super-property path.

description.new:
Own the full raw Test262 `language` gap for Lyng JS, including both in-scope ECMA-262 failures and currently skipped proposal coverage. This epic tracks the remaining work needed to move the language slice from the April 23, 2026 baseline to zero unexplained failures and zero skips.

Baseline evidence:
- `cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter language --report /tmp/lyng-js-language.md -j 4` produced 23,637 selected, 22,768 runnable, 22,005 passed, 763 failed, 869 skipped.
- A repeat `--list-failures` run reported 761 failures, so there is minor run-to-run variance; treat the April 23, 2026 /tmp artifacts as the analysis source of truth.

This epic intentionally includes proposal-driven skips under `language`, not just Phase 6 core semantics, because the goal is to clear the raw language pass-rate gap to 100%.

notes.new: Use the /tmp April 23, 2026 language artifacts and the checked-in Test262 harness/reporting flow as the baseline. No dependencies should be added between child issues unless implementation sequencing later proves necessary.

notes.old: Current analysis counted roughly 54 explicit `abrupt completion` failures and a broader operator exactness tail nearby. Keep this issue focused on semantic exactness in `ops`/`vm`, not parser or lowering-only gaps.
notes.new:
Current analysis counted roughly 54 explicit abrupt completion failures and a broader operator exactness tail nearby. Keep this issue focused on semantic exactness in ops/vm, not parser or lowering-only gaps.

Implementation summary: BigInt bitwise operators now use ToNumeric with BigInt operands; relational comparison uses ToPrimitive, StringToBigInt, ToNumeric, and exact BigInt/Number ordering; ordinary ToPrimitive skips non-callable valueOf/toString candidates; computed member assignment prepares ToPropertyKey once; global reference reads use accessor-aware property access; dynamic reference target resolution uses HasProperty without invoking getters. A small Temporal parser policy correction was included so lyng-js-ops verification stays clean against checked-in Test262 expectations.

Verification 2026-04-23: cargo fmt --all exited 0; cargo test -p lyng-js-ops passed 74/74; cargo test -p lyng-js-compiler passed 48/48; cargo test -p lyng-js-vm -p lyng-js-tests passed 810/810 and 232/232; Test262 language/expressions/bitwise-and passed 30/30; language/expressions/less-than passed 45/45; language/expressions/compound-assignment passed 454/454.

description.new:
Own the full Test262 `built-ins` pass-rate gap for Lyng JS, including both runnable failures and selected skipped cases. Skips are in scope because the selected pass rate counts them against completion.

Baseline evidence from April 24, 2026:
- `cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter built-ins --report /tmp/lyng-js-builtins.md -j 12` produced 23,388 selected, 21,439 runnable, 16,614 passed, 4,825 failed, 1,949 skipped, 0 panics, 71.04% selected pass rate.
- `cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter built-ins --report /tmp/lyng-js-builtins-list.md --list-failures -j 12` produced the raw 4,825-failure inventory used for clustering.

Scope:
- In scope: ECMA-262 built-ins, non-Intl Temporal built-ins, Stage 3+ built-in feature skips selected by the current runner policy, built-ins harness/bootstrap/runtime-policy skips that count against selected pass rate.
- Out of scope: ECMA-402 `intl402/*`, below-Stage-3 proposal exclusions, and web/Node host APIs.

Primary owner crates and tools: `crates/lyng-js/builtins`, `crates/lyng-js/ops`, `crates/lyng-js/objects`, `crates/lyng-js/env`, `tools/lyng-js-test262`. Keep fixes in the owning semantic layer rather than duplicating abstract operations inside builtins.

acceptance.new:

description.new:
Close TypedArray, ArrayBuffer, SharedArrayBuffer, DataView, and binary text-conversion built-ins gaps from the April 24, 2026 built-ins baseline.

Baseline evidence:
- Full slice command: `cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter built-ins --report /tmp/lyng-js-builtins.md -j 12`
- Built-ins baseline: 23,388 selected, 16,614 passed, 4,825 failed, 1,949 skipped.
- Raw failure inventory shows TypedArrayConstructors 110, DataView 78, TypedArray 11, Uint8Array base64/hex 60, ArrayBuffer 2, SharedArrayBuffer 2.
- Skips include resizable-arraybuffer 450, arraybuffer-transfer 41, oversize Array.prototype.slice guard 4, and oversize ArrayBuffer allocation guard 2.

Major clusters:
- `built-ins/TypedArrayConstructors/internals`: 69 failures.
- DataView BigInt/float16/get/set clusters: 78 failures.
- `Uint8Array.fromBase64/fromHex`, `Uint8Array.prototype.setFromBase64/setFromHex`, `toBase64`, and `toHex`: 60 failures.
- Large allocation and length-excessive tests currently time out or abort.

Example failures:
- `built-ins/TypedArrayConstructors/ctors/object-arg/length-excessive-throws.js: timeout after 1.0s`
- `built-ins/Uint8Array/fromBase64/results.js: runtime error: TypeError`
- `built-ins/DataView/prototype/getBigInt64/` cluster failures from the raw list.

Likely owner areas: typed-array and DataView builtins in `crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch.rs`, backing-store model in `crates/lyng-js/objects`, shared-memory ops, and `docs/lyng-js/shared-memory-and-backing-stores.md` if ownership/status changes.

description.new:
Create a P2 readiness epic for Lyng JS JIT tiering without pulling native-code execution ahead of the current P1 ECMA-262/Test262 completion work.

Current state as of April 24, 2026:
- No existing dcat issues match `jit` or `feedback`.
- Lyng JS docs currently mark JIT code generation as out of scope for core completion.
- Existing JIT-enabling substrate includes immutable `CodeRef` templates, compile-time feedback descriptors, lazy feedback vectors, property inline-cache state, object-owned invalidation epochs, safepoint/deoptimization metadata shells, and runtime/density benchmark tooling.
- The checked-in whole-suite Test262 report is still at 76.52% selected pass rate with open P1 conformance epics, so native-code execution should stay behind conformance stabilization.

In scope: JIT readiness design, tiering architecture, feedback and hotness hardening, safepoint/deopt validation, backend research, benchmark/report extensions, and future feature gating.

Out of scope: shipping native-code execution, optimizing compiler work, ECMA-402/web/Node APIs, and foundational rewrites to `Value`, handles, object layout, environments, bytecode encoding, or frame layout without a separate approved architecture change.

Future decisions to track include per-`CodeRef` tier state and hotness counters, stable feedback inspection APIs, safepoint/deopt verification hooks, and a feature-gated native-code crate or backend integration point. Backend choice is deferred to the backend research child issue.

acceptance.new:
Child issues cover architecture, tiering, feedback/IC hardening, safepoint/deopt validation, backend research, benchmarks/reports, and feature gating.
The epic remains P2/open and no issue is marked in_progress during creation.
`dcat list --agent-only`, `dcat list <epic-id>`, and `dcat show <epic-id>` show the parent and full child breakdown.
The epic explicitly preserves current P1 ECMA-262/Test262 completion work as higher priority than native-code implementation.

description.new:
Define the interpreter-side tiering contract needed before JIT work can start. The key design is per-`CodeRef` state for hotness, tier eligibility, tier status, invalidation, and future native-code attachment, without compiling or executing native code.

Relevant code: `crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm.rs`, `crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm/feedback.rs`, `crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm/install.rs`, `crates/lyng-js/vm/src/installed.rs`, and `crates/lyng-js/vm/src/frame.rs`.

The contract must preserve shared feedback semantics for closures that share one `CodeRef` and must keep guest-visible execution identical to interpreter-only execution.

acceptance.new:

notes.new: Suggested child issues: builtin registry/bootstrap extraction; proxy-aware object operation surface; dynamic-compilation service extraction; clippy/readability baseline for builtins; owner-layer tests for proxy/eval/bootstrap invariants. Do not close this epic without explicit user approval.

description.new:
Create a dedicated quality-recovery track for the Lyng JS engine. Recent Test262 conformance work has improved coverage, but modularity and readability have regressed enough that future conformance fixes are becoming harder to review safely.

Review findings to preserve:

1. Builtin bootstrap/dispatch is too large to review safely. `crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public.rs:2226` contains `ensure_public_realm_builtins`, a 10k+ line bootstrap function. It sits beside large hand-maintained object lookup, metadata, and dispatch chains. Builtin fixes currently require synchronized edits across IDs, handles, install code, metadata, and dispatch.

2. Abstract operation ownership is split. `crates/lyng-js/ops/src/object.rs:703` has a `Get` path that directly enters object storage while proxy-trap-aware paths live elsewhere. Correctness depends on callers choosing the right helper, which conflicts with the documented architecture goal that abstract operations have one owner and one semantic surface.

3. Dynamic compilation is VM-owned. `crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm/builtin_dispatch.rs:758` and nearby VM code implement Function constructor compilation, indirect eval, direct eval, and script evaluation as separate VM flows. The docs call for one shared dynamic-compilation service/cache contract.

4. The documented clippy gate currently fails. `crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/temporal.rs:1965` contains a real Temporal comparison bug: `temporal_exact_time_unit_order` returns `u8`, so comparing it with `<= 0` is only true for zero. The JS engine also has hundreds of pedantic warnings, mostly concentrated in builtins.

The goal is not a broad rewrite. The goal is to establish guardrails, extract canonical ownership points, and keep Test262 progress while reducing review risk.

acceptance.new: A fresh agent can start quality-recovery work from this issue without needing the original review thread. Acceptance requires: (1) the current hard clippy failure is fixed or explicitly tracked as a child issue; (2) a canonical builtin registry/bootstrap/dispatch plan exists or is implemented incrementally without changing builtin IDs; (3) proxy-aware object operation ownership is consolidated behind one `lyng-js-ops` surface; (4) Function constructor, direct eval, indirect eval, and script evaluation share a documented dynamic-compilation service/cache contract; (5) owner-layer tests cover extracted behavior before or alongside refactors; (6) focused Test262 slices are run for any semantics touched.

description.new: Parent epic for the comprehensive Lyng JS code review findings from April 25 2026. The review identified guest-triggered panic risk, panic-based compiler and bytecode limits, unclear proxy-aware object operation boundaries, collectible bootstrap atoms, VM ownership of spec-like builtin semantics, and oversized core modules. This epic groups the remediation work without marking any child as started.

acceptance.new: All child review findings are either fixed with focused verification, explicitly deferred with rationale, or superseded by more specific issues. Documentation and tests reflect the final ownership and safety contracts.

title: Address Lyng JS code quality review findings

title.new: Separate proxy-aware object operations from ordinary-only helpers

acceptance.new: Ordinary-only helpers are renamed, scoped, or documented so they cannot be mistaken for proxy-observable ECMAScript operations. Guest-observable VM and builtin paths use proxy-aware context operations unless an ordinary-only path is explicitly required by the spec algorithm. Focused tests cover representative proxy traps for get, has, set, deleteProperty, defineProperty, getOwnPropertyDescriptor, ownKeys, getPrototypeOf, and setPrototypeOf through affected VM and builtin paths.

notes.new: Primary evidence: crates/lyng-js/ops/src/object.rs:838-879 direct public helpers. Proxy-aware counterparts live around object.rs:99-234. ObjectRuntime Proxy forwarding appears in crates/lyng-js/objects/src/internal_methods.rs around get_prototype_of line 17, get_own_property line 132, define_own_property line 168, has_property line 213, get line 241, set line 272, delete line 306, own_property_keys line 340. Review probe showed Array.prototype.indexOf.call(proxy, ...) currently passes through the public builtin path, so this is a boundary and auditability issue rather than that specific failing behavior.

description.new: Finding from April 25 2026 Lyng JS code review. crates/lyng-js/ops/src/object.rs exposes proxy-aware *_in_context operations next to exported helpers with ECMAScript names such as has_property and get that go directly to ObjectRuntime internal methods. ObjectRuntime proxy branches forward to the proxy target without traps, so these helpers are ordinary-only in practice but easy to call as if they were spec operations.

title: Separate proxy-aware object operations from ordinary-only helpers

description.new: Finding from April 25 2026 Lyng JS code review. crates/lyng-js/bytecode/src/builder.rs still models register, operand, instruction stream, jump, constant, child-function, capture, exception-handler, feedback, and safepoint limits with expect or panic. These limits are ultimately controlled by source size or program shape and should produce structured lowering or compile errors rather than aborting the process.

notes.new: Primary evidence: crates/lyng-js/bytecode/src/builder.rs:218-222 alloc_register panics. Related evidence: emit_abc around line 317, emit_call around line 403, emit_tail_call around line 425, emit_construct around line 443, patch_jump_to around line 457, add_constant/add_child_function/add_capture/add_exception_handler around lines 501-535. Compiler has LoweringError::RegisterOverflow but builder APIs are not uniformly fallible.

acceptance.new: Public bytecode builder APIs used by compiler lowering are fallible for source-size and program-shape limits. Compiler plumbs these failures into structured lowering diagnostics. Regression tests cover at least register overflow, narrow call operand overflow, jump distance or instruction stream overflow where practical, and oversized constant or child-function tables where practical. No guest-triggerable panic remains for these limits.

title.new: Make bytecode builder source limits fallible

title: Make bytecode builder source limits fallible

description.new: Finding from April 25 2026 Lyng JS code review. Several core Lyng JS files are thousands of lines and mix multiple major responsibilities, contrary to the repo-level Rust module guidance. This makes ownership, review, and targeted verification harder.

title.new: Split oversized Lyng JS core modules along ownership boundaries

acceptance.new: Create an incremental module split plan that preserves public APIs and crate boundaries. Split at least the highest-impact files by domain or object family without broad semantic rewrites. Keep lib.rs and main.rs thin, expose intentional public APIs via pub use, and run targeted tests for every moved subsystem.

title: Split oversized Lyng JS core modules along ownership boundaries

title.new: Move spec-like builtin semantics out of VM builtin dispatch

acceptance.new: Spec-like Object, Array, and String builtin algorithms live in lyng-js-builtins or lyng-js-ops rather than VM builtin dispatch. VM-owned dispatch remains limited to execution-sensitive services such as direct eval, call bridging, and bytecode-specific machinery. Tests demonstrate that internal shims and public builtins share the same semantic implementation or explicit delegated helper.

notes.new: Primary evidence: crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm/builtin_dispatch.rs:1102-1118 implements object_has_own_property_builtin. Additional examples include array_index_of_builtin around line 1152, array_push_builtin around line 1290, array_pop_builtin around line 1330, and object_to_string_builtin around line 1372. Internal shims are allocated in crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/internal.rs:337-347 and dispatched through InternalBuiltinDispatchContext around internal.rs:954.

description.new: Finding from April 25 2026 Lyng JS code review. VM builtin dispatch still implements Object, Array, and String spec-like algorithms and internal shim behavior directly. The Lyng JS architecture says builtins and ops own semantic algorithms while the VM executes bytecode and provides narrow execution services. Keeping spec-visible behavior in the VM increases drift risk against public builtin implementations.

title: Move spec-like builtin semantics out of VM builtin dispatch

title.new: Intern bootstrap builtin descriptor atoms as permanent metadata

notes.new: Primary evidence: crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/families/arrays.rs:392-437 uses atoms.intern_collectible for Array descriptor names. AtomTable lifetime contract is documented at crates/lyng-js/common/src/atom.rs:7-9, with permanent intern at atom.rs:431 and collectible intern at atom.rs:435. Similar collectible bootstrap names should be audited across builtins family installers and internal builtin metadata.

acceptance.new: Bootstrap descriptor names, builtin metadata names, and realm-owned spec names use permanent atoms or are promoted to permanent during bootstrap. Runtime guest-created strings and property keys continue to use collectible atomization where appropriate. Add a focused test or invariant that realm/bootstrap descriptor atoms remain permanent after bootstrap and after an atom sweep.

description.new: Finding from April 25 2026 Lyng JS code review. Bootstrap and public builtin descriptor names are interned with intern_collectible even though AtomTable documents permanent frontend and metadata atoms versus collectible runtime atomization. Immutable realm metadata should not depend on collectible atom liveness tracing and should not add avoidable GC sweep work.

description.new:
Lyng JS keeps Annex B inside core ECMA-262 completion and active 6H work. The checked-in reports/js/lyng-js/test262.md currently shows annexB at 1377 selected, 1305 runnable, 225 passed, 1080 failed, 72 skipped, and 16.34% selected pass rate.

No direct dcat tracker exists for Annex B. Existing search only finds an incidental String.prototype.substr note on Temporal issue lyng-55no.

Track closure of Annex B language, parser/runtime, and builtin behavior against Test262. Known current clusters include annexB/language/eval-code/direct and annexB/language/eval-code/indirect SyntaxError failures, annexB/language/global-code and annexB/language/function-code MissingDeclarationBinding lowering failures, and annexB/built-ins/unescape ReferenceError failures. Existing partial support includes lexer HTML-like comments in crates/lyng-js/lexer/src/lexer.rs and parser Annex B call-assignment-target handling in crates/lyng-js/parser/src/parser.rs.

Out of scope: ECMA-402 Intl, web or Node host APIs, and unrelated proposal work.

description.new:
Implement Annex B block-level function declaration semantics for global and function code.

Scope includes ECMA-262 Annex B.3.3-style behavior for sloppy-mode block function declarations, declaration collection, binding creation and initialization, and interactions with lexical/var/function declarations. This task owns the current annexB/language/global-code and annexB/language/function-code MissingDeclarationBinding lowering failures shown in reports/js/lyng-js/test262.md.

Keep work in the owning crates: sema for declaration/binding analysis, compiler for lowering, env for environment behavior, and VM for execution semantics. Do not hide binding policy inside unrelated builtin or harness code.

This task owns the current annexB/language/eval-code/direct and annexB/language/eval-code/indirect SyntaxError, ReferenceError, and Test262 assertion clusters visible in reports/js/lyng-js/test262.md.

Existing partial support includes HTML-like comments in crates/lyng-js/lexer/src/lexer.rs and Annex B call-assignment-target handling in crates/lyng-js/parser/src/parser.rs, so new work should extend those ownership points rather than duplicating syntax policy elsewhere.

title.new: Add --shell print global to lyng-js CLI

description.new: lyng-js currently keeps the CLI embedding spec-only and does not expose harness, browser, or Node-style globals. The V8/v8-v7 benchmark in /Users/sondre/dev/js-engine-benchmark uses print(...) and only falls back to console.log, so lyng-js cannot run it today. Add an opt-in --shell CLI flag that installs a print(...) global while preserving default strict behavior.

notes.new: Implementation scope: add crates/lyng-js/cli/src/extensions.rs with a minimal CliRealmExtension provider, wire shell_mode through CliInvocation and parse_args, and route script/module execution through the VM extension entry points only when --shell is present.

acceptance.old: cargo test -p lyng-js-cli passes, and a release build can run the documented print smoke checks.
acceptance.new: Default lyng-js <script> still leaves print undefined and reports ReferenceError for print(1+1). lyng-js --shell <script> installs global print(...), converts each argument through VM string conversion, joins arguments with spaces, writes a trailing newline to stdout, and exits successfully. --shell is accepted only before the script path; trailing --shell after the script path is rejected as an unexpected trailing argument. Script and module execution paths use the shell extension only when shell mode is enabled. cargo test -p lyng-js-cli passes, and a release build can run the documented print smoke checks.

description.new: Prototype a future Lyng JS native baseline backend using Cranelift only after the JIT-readiness substrate has explicit tiering, safepoint/root-map, callout, reporting, and feature-gating contracts. This issue must not add Cranelift to the default workspace dependency set. The spike should be non-default, either behind a Cargo feature or isolated prototype crate, and should treat macOS/aarch64 plus x86_64 execution-memory constraints as acceptance blockers before executable code lands.

title.new: Spike non-default Cranelift baseline backend

acceptance.new:
- Cranelift is not a default workspace dependency and cannot affect normal builds unless an explicit non-default feature/prototype target is selected.
- The spike lowers only a tiny straight-line bytecode subset such as Smi arithmetic and return.
- Slow paths go through a documented VM/runtime callout ABI instead of reaching across crate internals.
- Safepoint/root-map policy is documented and tested; if the first subset forbids GC-triggering calls, that restriction is explicit and enforced.
- macOS/aarch64 and x86_64 executable-memory feasibility notes are recorded before native code is enabled.
- Benchmark/report output compares interpreter, bytecode-specialized, and native-spike compile/run costs.

title.new: Fix derived constructor lexical environment depth for language super object-spread failures

description.new: Focused child of lyng-2ksr for the Test262 language/expressions/super MissingEnvironment cluster. Reproduction: a derived class constructor that reads a top-level lexical binding, including Test262 cases such as language/expressions/super/call-spread-sngl-obj-ident.js and super({...o}), fails with script execution failed: MissingEnvironment(EnvironmentRef(3)). Root cause evidence: explicit derived constructors are forced to allocate a function environment at runtime, but compile-time environment-depth calculation in crates/lyng-js/compiler/src/script/state.rs does not count that derived-constructor environment, so outer lexical accesses are emitted one environment level too shallow. Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/compiler/src/script/state.rs, crates/lyng-js/compiler/src/script/function.rs, crates/lyng-js/tests/src/execution_semantics/classes.rs, reports/js/lyng-js/test262.md. Acceptance: add a focused regression for derived constructors reading outer lexical bindings with object spread; fix environment-depth accounting so the regression passes; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests covers the regression; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter language/expressions/super --report /tmp/lyng-js-super.md -j 4 reports 0 failed and 0 panicked variants for the super slice.

title: Fix derived constructor lexical environment depth for language super object-spread failures
Fix derived constructor lexical environment depth for language super object-spread failures

notes.new: Implemented and verified. Root cause: CompilationState::function_allocates_environment did not count explicit derived-class constructor environments even though function lowering marks those bytecode functions as needs_environment. This made outer lexical accesses in derived constructors use depth 0 instead of depth 1 and produced MissingEnvironment(EnvironmentRef(3)) for Test262 language/expressions/super object-spread cases. Fix: mirrored the derived-constructor/class-constructor environment allocation rules in crates/lyng-js/compiler/src/script/state.rs. Regression coverage: compiler test compile_script_counts_forced_derived_constructor_environment_in_capture_depths and runtime test phase6_derived_constructors_resolve_outer_lexical_bindings_with_forced_environment. Verification: cargo fmt --all passed; cargo test -p lyng-js-compiler passed 58 tests plus doc-tests; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests passed 1068 tests plus doc-tests; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter language/expressions/super --report /tmp/lyng-js-super.md -j 4 passed 94/94 files and 184/184 variants with 0 failed, 0 panicked, 0 skipped; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter language --report /tmp/lyng-js-language.md -j 4 now reports 23631 passed files, 1 failed file, 0 panicked, 0 skipped, and 44337 passed variants, 2 failed variants.

title: Fix derived constructor lexical environment depth for language super object-spread failures

title.new: Optimize Lyng JS RegExp performance

description.new:
Track the RegExp performance work found during the May 3, 2026 deep analysis of the Lyng JS RegExp implementation. Current implementation shape: lexer/parser validate RegExp literals in crates/lyng-js/parser/src/regexp.rs, compiler lowers literals through internal_regexp_literal_builtin in crates/lyng-js/compiler/src/script/expr.rs, runtime RegExp objects store RegExpPayload side-table records in crates/lyng-js/objects/src/regexp.rs and crates/lyng-js/objects/src/runtime.rs, and builtins execute RegExp.prototype.exec/test plus RegExp string-symbol methods in crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp.rs and regexp/symbols.rs. The general matcher delegates to regress 0.11.1 with UTF-16 support, plus local fast-path shims for selected current conformance and generated-test hot cases.

Analysis evidence from this workspace: cargo test -p lyng-js-objects regexp passed 7/7; cargo test -p lyng-js-parser regexp passed 12/12; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests regexp passed 34/34; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter built-ins/RegExp/CharacterClassEscapes --report /tmp/lyng-js-test262-regexp-charclass.md -j 4 passed 24/24 variants in 1.6s; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-bench -- runtime --report /tmp/lyng-js-bench-regexp-analysis.md reported regexp-heavy.runtime at 55,911 ns/work-unit, the slowest runtime row. Checked-in reports/js/lyng-js/test262.md also shows RegExp generated tests dominating the slowest Test262 table while built-ins/language RegExp are currently green.

Goal: reduce real RegExp runtime and generated-test cost without weakening ECMA-262 behavior, proxy-observable semantics, lastIndex behavior, or Annex B compatibility. Prefer targeted, measured changes over a broad matcher rewrite.

title: Optimize Lyng JS RegExp performance

title.new: Improve RegExp payload memory accounting and benchmark coverage

description.new:
RegExpPayload::payload_bytes in crates/lyng-js/objects/src/regexp.rs currently charges source text, source_units, flag text, and size_of::<regress::Regex>. The regress compiled regex internally owns instruction vectors, bracket tables, group-name boxes, and other heap data, so current RegExp payload accounting likely undercounts retained memory. The runtime benchmark has one regexp-heavy.runtime row in tools/lyng-js-bench/src/runtime.rs, but it mixes global exec, sticky tests, match indices, named captures, and replacement, making it hard to attribute gains.

Improve observability before or alongside performance implementation. Add or refine RegExp benchmark rows that isolate compile/literal creation, repeated default exec, global replacement with named captures, generated class/property scans, and legacy static accessor reads. Improve RegExp payload accounting if regress exposes enough structure; otherwise document the known lower-bound accounting and add explicit report language.

Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/objects/src/regexp.rs, crates/lyng-js/objects/src/runtime.rs, crates/lyng-js/env/src/accounting.rs, tools/lyng-js-bench/src/runtime.rs, reports/js/lyng-js/bench.md.

title.new: Streamline RegExp validation and backend normalization

acceptance.new: Record timing before/after for at least one generated RegExp Test262 filter and explain any remaining timeout exceptions.

description.new:
RegExp validation in crates/lyng-js/parser/src/regexp.rs performs multiple full-pattern passes and allocates HashMap/HashSet/String structures for capture counting, duplicate named group validation, named-group collection, and named-reference validation. RegExpPayload::compile in crates/lyng-js/objects/src/regexp.rs then normalizes backend patterns with character Vecs and a chain of String::replace calls for Unicode property aliases and legacy handling. The checked-in Test262 report shows generated RegExp and property-escape tests among the slowest files, so compile/validation overhead matters even when runtime matching is correct.

Refactor validation/normalization to reduce repeated scans and allocations while staying spec-traceable. Candidate work: combine capture counting/name collection/reference validation into one parser-state pass, avoid building temporary inner Strings for braced quantifiers and escapes where slices/ranges suffice, replace chained property-alias String::replace with a single scanner, and keep fast paths for patterns without named groups/property escapes/astraI code points. Preserve the current conformance shims and error behavior.

Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/parser/src/regexp.rs, crates/lyng-js/parser/src/regexp_tables.rs, crates/lyng-js/objects/src/regexp.rs, tools/lyng-js-test262/src/lib.rs timeout handling.

title: Streamline RegExp validation and backend normalization

acceptance.new: Regenerate runtime benchmark evidence and record regexp-heavy.runtime before/after.

description.new:
RegExp builtin symbol methods route through regexp_exec in crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp.rs. That helper gets the receiver exec property and, when callable, calls it to completion before falling back to regexp_exec_state. RegExp.prototype.test has a narrow default-exec shortcut, but @@match, @@replace, @@search, @@split, @@matchAll, and internal helper loops often materialize full JS match arrays even when the current builtin exec function is unchanged and the caller only needs match state, position, captures, or a boolean.

Add a shared fast path for the default built-in RegExp.prototype.exec case. The path should call regexp_exec_state directly and materialize a JS match result only when the ECMAScript algorithm requires the observable result object. It must preserve proxy/subclass/custom-exec behavior: if exec is overridden, non-callable, accessor-observable, or cross-realm behavior requires the public path, keep the current property/call semantics.

Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp.rs, crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp/symbols.rs, crates/lyng-js/tests/src/execution_semantics/script_core.rs.

title.new: Reduce UTF-16 allocation in RegExp execution loops

description.new:
RegExp.prototype.exec/test and the RegExp string-symbol methods repeatedly call string_ref_code_units in crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/support.rs, producing a fresh Vec<u16> for a StringRef. In hot loops such as while ((m = re.exec(str)) !== null), the same input string can be copied for every builtin entry and sometimes again for matched/capture strings. This adds allocation and memory traffic on top of matcher cost.

Investigate and implement a lower-allocation input path for RegExp execution. Options include a borrowed string code-unit view, a reusable scratch buffer scoped to the builtin call/VM execution, or an object/runtime helper that exposes Latin-1/UTF-16 data without forcing Vec allocation. Preserve correct handling of Latin-1, UTF-16, lone surrogates, and values produced by ToString. Do not introduce references that outlive heap mutation safety.

Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/support.rs, crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp.rs, crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp/symbols.rs, crates/lyng-js/gc string storage APIs.

title: Reduce UTF-16 allocation in RegExp execution loops

acceptance.new: Benchmark runtime and, if cache memory is retained, update accounting/reporting to make retained RegExp payload memory visible.

description.new:
RegExp literals are currently lowered in crates/lyng-js/compiler/src/script/expr.rs by loading pattern and flags strings and calling internal_regexp_literal_builtin. The builtin validates and compiles a new RegExpPayload each time the literal expression is evaluated. ECMAScript requires a fresh RegExp object for each literal evaluation, but the compiled matcher payload can be cached per script/function template or agent-level pattern/flags key and cloned/reused into fresh objects if ownership and mutability are handled correctly.

Design and implement a cache for compiled RegExp payloads that avoids repeated parser validation/backend compilation for hot literal sites. Keep the object allocation fresh per evaluation, preserve lastIndex initialization, preserve source/source_units/flag text, and ensure the cache is scoped so realm/agent lifetime and memory accounting remain correct. Avoid global process-level mutable caches.

Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/compiler/src/script/expr.rs, crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp.rs, crates/lyng-js/objects/src/regexp.rs, crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm/dynamic_compilation.rs, crates/lyng-js/env/src/agent/accounting.rs.

title.new: Make RegExp legacy static state lazy

description.new:
Every successful internal RegExp match currently calls record_regexp_legacy_static_match in crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp.rs, which calls RegExpLegacyStaticState::record_match in crates/lyng-js/env/src/execution.rs. That eagerly copies the full input, last match, left context, right context, and up to nine capture strings on every successful match. In global/sticky loops this can become O(input length * match count), even when Annex B legacy static accessors such as RegExp.input, RegExp.lastMatch, RegExp.leftContext, RegExp.rightContext, RegExp., and aliases are never read.

Implement a lazy representation for the realm RegExp legacy static state: retain enough source identity/ranges/capture ranges to materialize legacy accessor strings only when the Annex B getters run, while preserving observable behavior when RegExp.input is explicitly set and when later matches overwrite prior state. Keep realm isolation and cross-realm legacy accessor checks intact.

Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp.rs, crates/lyng-js/env/src/execution.rs, crates/lyng-js/tests/src/execution_semantics/script_core.rs.

notes.new: Implemented lazy RegExp legacy static text state with Empty/Owned/SourceSlice backing. Internal RegExp matches now record the input StringRef plus source ranges for input, lastMatch, leftContext, rightContext, lastParen, and - instead of eagerly copying code-unit slices on every successful match. Annex B getters materialize strings only when read, RegExp.input setter still stores explicit owned input without clearing prior match state, and agent GC tracing now roots source strings retained by lazy legacy static state. Added env/unit coverage for source-range recording, a no-accessor global-loop regression that verifies lazy state, and a forced-GC multiple-match accessor regression. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check passed; cargo test -p lyng-js-env passed 39 tests plus 0 doctests; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests regexp passed 37 tests; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter annexB/built-ins/RegExp --report /tmp/lyng-js-test262-annexb-regexp-lazy-statics.md -j 4 passed 62 files / 124 variants with 0 failures, 0 skips, and 0 panics. Benchmark: cargo run --release -p lyng-js-bench -- runtime --report /tmp/lyng-js-bench-regexp-lazy-statics.md measured regexp-heavy.runtime at 57,199.90 ns/work-unit; previous /tmp/lyng-js-bench-regexp-cache.md baseline was 55,709.99 ns/work-unit (+2.7%). The generic runtime row does not isolate large-input legacy-static copying, but this change removes the previous per-match eager full-input/context/capture copies when legacy statics are not read.

notes.new: Implemented targeted RegExp validation/normalization allocation reductions. Parser validation now builds the pattern char buffer once and reuses it for capture counting and validation, validates flags with a u8 bitset instead of a HashSet, and skips duplicate-name/name-collection passes unless the pattern may contain named captures. Backend normalization now uses a single Unicode-property alias scanner that borrows unchanged patterns and allocates only on actual alias rewrites, replacing the prior chain of full String::replace passes. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check; cargo test -p lyng-js-parser regexp (12 passed); cargo test -p lyng-js-objects regexp (8 passed; one existing timing-threshold test initially exceeded 27ms, then passed alone and on full rerun); Test262 built-ins/RegExp/property-escapes/generated before 97.1s 938/938, after 96.4s 938/938; Test262 built-ins/RegExp/CharacterClassEscapes 24/24 in 1.4s. No timeout exceptions were observed in the targeted filters.

acceptance.new: Production allocation paths have an explicit root source when collection can run. Active VM registers, frames, current_exception, and retained VM object/value caches are traced or otherwise rooted during GC. Add focused tests that force collection while values are live on the VM stack and in VM-owned caches. Document the ownership boundary between Agent roots and VM execution roots.

description.new: Review finding: PrimitiveMutator only collects when constructed with a PrimitiveRoots set, but production allocation paths use heap.mutator(), so maybe_collect_for_growth returns before collecting. Vm owns live Values and ObjectRefs in register_stack, current_exception, template_cache, dynamic caches, async and generator tables, while AgentCollectionSnapshot traces only Agent-owned state. This means normal allocation growth does not collect today, and enabling collection without a VM root source could collect live VM state. Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/gc/src/mutator.rs, crates/lyng-js/env/src/agent.rs, crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm.rs.

title.new: Include VM-owned residency in runtime memory accounting and reports

description.new: Review finding: RuntimePhase6Accounting reports Agent heap domains, RegExp payloads, literal cache, promise jobs, module placeholders, and backing stores, but not Vm-owned memory. Vm owns installed functions, feedback vectors, tiering state, source_texts, atom_texts, template_cache, dynamic_function_cache, suspended/async/generator side tables, and scratch buffers. Current bench snapshots call runtime.phase6_accounting(), so long-lived VM residency and cache growth are invisible. Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/env/src/accounting.rs, crates/lyng-js/env/src/agent/accounting.rs, crates/lyng-js/env/src/runtime.rs, crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm.rs, tools/lyng-js-bench/src/runtime.rs.

acceptance.new: Expose Vm-owned memory accounting or an engine-level accounting snapshot that includes both Runtime/Agent and Vm residency. Runtime benchmark reports include installed code, feedback/tiering, source/atom text caches, template/dynamic caches, and async/generator side-state where applicable. Add a fixture that keeps a Vm alive while reporting memory so these domains are visible. Document any intentionally excluded transient scratch capacity.

title: Include VM-owned residency in runtime memory accounting and reports

description.new: Review finding: AgentCluster and cluster handles use Rc<RefCell<BackingStoreRuntime>> and Rc<RefCell<SharedMemoryRuntime>>, while shared backing-store bytes use Arc<Mutex<Vec<u8>>>. Typed-array storage helpers read and write byte by byte through backing_store_get_byte and backing_store_set_byte, causing repeated RefCell borrows and mutex locks for multi-byte elements. This conflicts with the architecture for cluster-owned cross-agent coordination and narrow typed atomic access. Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/env/src/cluster.rs, crates/lyng-js/env/src/agent/cluster_handles.rs, crates/lyng-js/env/src/backing_store.rs, crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/binary_data/typed_arrays.rs.

acceptance.new: Shared backing-store ownership is suitable for the intended cross-agent host boundary or the remaining single-thread limitation is explicitly documented and tracked. Typed-array read/write helpers use element-width or slice-level access instead of byte-at-a-time public calls in hot paths. Atomics use a narrow typed access boundary with alignment and bounds checks above it. Add focused benchmarks or report rows for typed-array read/write/fill/copy and Atomics operations before and after.

acceptance.new: Direct and indirect eval analysis policy lives in sema/compiler-owned APIs rather than VM-local ScriptSema mutation helpers. Existing direct eval, indirect eval, Function constructor, and evalScript behavior is preserved with focused tests. Update docs if the dynamic compilation ownership contract changes.

title.new: Move dynamic eval sema rewriting out of the VM crate

description.new: Review finding: crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm/dynamic_compilation.rs imports parser and sema directly, analyzes dynamic source, rewrites ScriptSema use sites and binding storage classes, and then compiles the result. This places dynamic parse/sema policy in the execution crate even though compiler::dynamic is the intended owner of dynamic source parse/sema/compile policy. The VM should provide caller and environment facts, then install/cache the compiled result. Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm/dynamic_compilation.rs, crates/lyng-js/compiler/src/dynamic.rs, crates/lyng-js/sema/src.

title: Move dynamic eval sema rewriting out of the VM crate

description.new: Review finding: InstalledFunction allocates multiple Vec<Option<_>> tables sized to function.instructions().len() for wide payloads, direct-eval sites, loop-iteration sites, feedback by offset, source maps, safepoints, and deopt metadata. Large functions with sparse metadata pay memory and install-time zeroing for every metadata class. This also affects future JIT and deoptimization metadata footprint. Relevant file: crates/lyng-js/vm/src/vm/install.rs.

description.new: Fresh staging baseline after c8bfca8 shows 143 failed files / 247 failed variants. The focused staging/sm/Symbol slice has 30 files / 56 variants with 3 failed files and 5 failed variants: constructor.js, conversions.js, and valueOf.js. Investigation shows two spec mismatches: Symbol.prototype is allocated as a Symbol wrapper carrying an anonymous symbol data slot, so Symbol.prototype.valueOf() does not throw; and the String constructor special-cases symbols after object ToPrimitive, so String(Object(Symbol())) returns a descriptive string instead of throwing TypeError per ToString(Symbol). Relevant files: crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/families/scaffolding.rs, crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/strings/basic.rs, and crates/lyng-js/tests/src/execution_semantics/script_core.rs.

description.new: Post-Symbol staging baseline is 140 failed files / 242 failed variants. The focused staging/sm/Set slice has 7 files / 14 variants with 3 failed files and 6 failed variants: union.js, intersection.js, and symmetric-difference.js. Root cause: Set.prototype.union and Set.prototype.symmetricDifference snapshot the receiver before acquiring the other set-like keys iterator next method, but staging expects the receiver copy after that observable next lookup. Set.prototype.intersection snapshots the receiver before set-like has calls, so it misses receiver mutations such as delete/re-add during iteration; staging expects live Set traversal behavior. Relevant code: crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/collections.rs and crates/lyng-js/tests/src/execution_semantics/script_core.rs.

title.new: Fix Iterator.from wrapper forwarding semantics

description.new: The regenerated whole-corpus Test262 report (reports/js/lyng-js/test262.md) leaves 144 staging files / 250 staging variants failing. The staging/sm/Iterator slice has 174 files with 11 failing files / 22 failing variants. The compact Iterator.from subgroup fails 5 files / 10 variants: modify-return.js, proxy-not-wrapped.js, proxy-wrap-next.js, proxy-wrap-return.js, and wrap-next-not-object-throws.js. Root-cause investigation points at crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/iterators.rs: Iterator.from currently represents WrapForValidIterator wrappers using the generic IteratorHelper object path. That path validates next results as objects, caches/uses helper done state for return, and walks prototypes with direct object storage instead of proxy-aware GetPrototypeOf. SpiderMonkey staging tests expect wrapper next to forward raw underlying next results, return to look up and call underlying return dynamically on each call, and Iterator.from(proxy over Iterator subclass) to use proxy-aware prototype lookup without wrapping the proxy.

title.new: Fix RegExp source re-escaping for split clones

description.new: The refreshed staging/sm/String split slice fails split-01.js and split-xregexp.js. Root-cause probe shows the split algorithm clones a regexp through RegExpSpeciesCreate/new RegExp(re, flags); RegExp.prototype.source currently re-escapes every slash in the stored source, including slashes already escaped in literal source text. A literal /<(\/)?([^<>]+)>/ reports source as <(\\/)?([^<>]+)> after cloning, so the clone matches as captures [undefined, /B] instead of [/, B].

title: Fix RegExp source re-escaping for split clones

The red run failed with Smi(24) vs Smi(31), showing cloned regex source and split captures were wrong while slash/newline source escaping still worked. Root cause was crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/regexp/accessors.rs escaping every slash in RegExp.prototype.source, including slashes already protected by an odd backslash run in literal source. Updated escape_regexp_pattern_units to escape only unprotected slashes. description.new: The refreshed whole Test262 report has the only built-ins failures in built-ins/Iterator/zip/iterables-iteration.js and built-ins/Iterator/zipKeyed/iterables-iteration.js. The tests require GetIteratorFlattenable to accept objects without Symbol.iterator as direct iterator records and only read their next property during Iterator.zip/zipKeyed collection. A local probe showed plain iterator/iterable inputs pass, but proxy-order cases containing an object without a callable next throw TypeError during Iterator.zip/zipKeyed construction. Root cause is crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/iterators.rs:get_iterator_flattenable eagerly requiring next to be callable, while ECMA-262 GetIteratorDirect stores the next method value without validating callability.

title.new: Defer Iterator.zip next-method validation

acceptance.new: Add regression coverage for Iterator.zip and Iterator.zipKeyed accepting direct iterator-like objects with missing/non-callable next during construction and preserving observable get order, update get_iterator_flattenable to defer next callability validation, and verify the built-ins/Iterator/zip plus zipKeyed Test262 slices.

title: Defer Iterator.zip next-method validation
Defer Iterator.zip next-method validation

notes.new: Root cause: Iterator.zip and Iterator.zipKeyed failed built-ins/Iterator/zip*/iterables-iteration.js because crates/lyng-js/builtins/src/public/dispatch/iterators.rs:get_iterator_flattenable eagerly required the direct iterator next property to be callable. ECMA-262 GetIteratorDirect only reads and stores next, so construction must accept direct iterator-like objects even when next is missing/non-callable; callability is checked later when stepping. Added script_core_iterator_zip_defers_direct_next_validation to cover Iterator.zip and Iterator.zipKeyed construction order through Proxy traps. description.new: The refreshed report shows staging/sm/strict with 14 failing variants, 13 of them strict-wrapper variants. The files include sm/non262-strict-shell.js, whose helpers intentionally run both lenient and strict code through eval/Function (testLenientAndStrict, returns, raisesException). The Test262 runner prepends "use strict" to the whole runtime source for strict variants before loading harness helpers, so those helper functions become strict and their supposedly lenient direct evals inherit strictness. Local probes show representative engine semantics for strict eval, strict assignment/delete, and direct eval strict inheritance already work; the failing strict variants are caused by running self-strictening SpiderMonkey tests under an outer strict wrapper.

title: Fix staging RegExp constructor failures

notes.new: Implemented RegExp constructor internal-slot and ordering fix. Red regression output before fix: script_core_regexp_constructor_uses_internal_slots_and_orders_new_target_lookup returned "bar|i|sourceflags|flagsprototype|a|g|true" instead of "foo|||prototypeflags|a|g|true". Root cause: RegExp constructor treated real RegExp objects like regexp-like objects by reading source/flags accessors, and it coerced flags before ordinary constructor prototype lookup. Fix: preserve internal source/flags for actual RegExp objects, carry pattern/flags seeds until after newTarget prototype lookup, then perform ToString/validation. title.new: Read RegExp flags after lastIndex ToLength side effects

description.new: SpiderMonkey staging failures staging/sm/RegExp/match-local-tolength-recompilation.js and staging/sm/RegExp/replace-local-tolength-recompilation.js mutate a RegExp from lastIndex.valueOf(). ECMA RegExpBuiltinExec observes lastIndex through ToLength before using current matcher flags/source; the current implementation reads flags before lastIndex conversion, so recompilation side effects leave stale global/sticky behavior and produce wrong lastIndex/match results.

notes.new: Root cause: staging/sm/RegExp/ignoreCase-non-latin1-to-latin1.js failed both variants with Test262Error. The regression first failed with left "true:true:true:true:true:true:false:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true", then after adding reverse non-unicode long-s checks failed with left "true:true:true:true:true:true:false:false:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true:true". This isolated backend over-folding of S/s and U+017F under plain /i, while Unicode-aware /iu literal folds still need ECMA-specific handling for the staging fold pairs. Fix adds a targeted captured-literal fast path for one-code-unit ignoreCase patterns and + forms, with separate legacy and Unicode-aware fold classes and correct quantified capture range. Verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-tests script_core_regexp_ignore_case_literal_folds_cross_latin1 => 1 passed after red; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/RegExp/ignoreCase-non-latin1-to-latin1.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-regexp-ignorecase-nonlatin1.md -j 4 => 1 file/2 variants passed, 0 failed; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests regexp => 49 passed; cargo test -p lyng-js-objects regexp => 8 passed; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/RegExp --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-staging-sm-regexp.md -j 4 => 87/91 files passed, 4 failed, 174/182 variants passed, 8 failed; cargo fmt --all; git diff --check.

title.new: Avoid partial surrogate RegExp backrefs

acceptance.new: Add regression coverage for /foo(.+)bar\1/u surrogate-boundary cases and the anchored /^(.+)\1$/u partial-pair case; implement targeted Unicode-aware backreference handling for these staging patterns without changing valid lone-surrogate matches; verify staging/sm/RegExp/unicode-back-reference.js plus focused regexp tests and the RegExp staging slice; update issue notes with command output before review.

description.new: staging/sm/RegExp/unicode-back-reference.js fails both strict and non-strict variants with Test262Error. The file checks Unicode-mode RegExp backreferences around surrogate boundaries: a backreference whose last code unit is a lead surrogate must not match the lead half of a real surrogate pair, and anchored repeated backreferences must not split surrogate pairs partially. Current execution delegates these backreference patterns to the backend, which allows partial surrogate-pair matches under /u.

title: Classify or fix staging RegExp harness parse errors
Classify or fix staging RegExp harness parse errors

notes.new: Root cause was lexer/parser template literal cooked storage treating valid UTF-16 surrogate escapes as invalid template escapes. Reproduced all three staging/sm/RegExp files before the fix with runtime error: harness parse error. Updated template scanning to preserve cooked UTF-16 code units for lone surrogate escapes and combine adjacent escaped surrogate pairs where they form a scalar; parser now stores UTF-16 cooked template quasis when needed. Regression coverage added in crates/lyng-js/lexer/src/tests.rs for escaped surrogate pairs and lone surrogate escapes in templates. Fresh verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-lexer template -- --nocapture => 7 passed; cargo test -p lyng-js-parser template -- --nocapture => 1 passed; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests regexp -- --nocapture => 51 passed; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/RegExp --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-staging-sm-regexp-after-1w3t.md -j 4 => 88/91 files passed, 3 failed, 0 panicked, 0 skipped. The former harness parse-error files now execute and fail as ordinary Test262Error cases: split-trace.js, unicode-class-raw.js, unicode-raw.js. Those semantic failures are separate follow-up work, not harness parse failures.

title: Fix staging String harness parse errors
Fix staging String harness parse errors

notes.new:
Reproduced on 2026-05-05: cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/String/at.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-string-at-parse-before.md -j 1 => 0/1 files passed, 2/2 variants failed with runtime error: harness parse error; unicode-braced.js with /tmp/lyng-js-string-unicode-braced-parse-before.md likewise failed 0/1 files, 2/2 variants with harness parse error. CLI diagnostics pointed at invalid unicode escape in string literals containing braced surrogate code points like \u{D800}.

Root cause: scan_string_unicode_escape_sequence accepted braced Unicode escapes only when char::from_u32(code) succeeded. That incorrectly rejected surrogate code points even though JS string literal semantics preserve them as UTF-16 code units. Added lexer regression string_unicode_braced_surrogate_escape_preserves_utf16, which failed red with [] vs [55296, 56320]. Updated the braced string escape path to return UnicodeStringEscape::CodeUnit for surrogate-range code points.

Fresh verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-lexer string_unicode_braced_surrogate_escape_preserves_utf16 -- --nocapture => 1 passed; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/String/at.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-string-at-after-1d3k.md -j 4 => 1/1 files, 2/2 variants passed; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/String/unicode-braced.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-string-unicode-braced-after-1d3k-default.md -j 1 => 1/1 files, 2/2 variants passed; cargo fmt --all; cargo test -p lyng-js-lexer string -- --nocapture => 17 passed; git diff --check.

title.new: Fix staging await identifier parse restrictions

title: Fix staging await identifier parse restrictions
Fix staging await identifier parse restrictions

notes.new:
Reproduced on 2026-05-05: staging/sm/fields/await-identifier-script.js failed both variants with runtime error: harness parse error in /tmp/lyng-js-await-identifier-script-before.md; staging/sm/fields/await-identifier-module-3.js and staging/sm/module/await-restricted-nested.js failed as expected parse error but frontend succeeded in /tmp/lyng-js-await-identifier-module-3-before.md and /tmp/lyng-js-await-restricted-nested-before.md. CLI diagnostics for await-identifier-script.js showed unexpected token Semicolon at the bare await field initializer.

Root cause: class field initializer values inherited the enclosing async parse context, so x = await; was parsed as an await expression and errored. Separately, module parsing still allowed await as an identifier reference inside nested non-async functions because primary expression parsing only checked allow_await, not module context. Added parser regressions class_field_initializer_does_not_inherit_async_await_context and await_is_restricted_inside_nested_module_functions. Red runs failed with the same unexpected Semicolon and missed module parse error. Fix parses class field initializer values with await/yield expression parsing disabled and prevents module code from treating await as an identifier reference.

Fresh verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-parser class_field_initializer_does_not_inherit_async_await_context -- --nocapture => 1 passed; cargo test -p lyng-js-parser await_is_restricted_inside_nested_module_functions -- --nocapture => 1 passed; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/fields/await-identifier-script.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-await-identifier-script-after-4j8l.md -j 4 => 1/1 files, 2/2 variants passed; await-identifier-module-3.js report /tmp/lyng-js-await-identifier-module-3-after-4j8l.md => 1/1 files passed; await-restricted-nested.js report /tmp/lyng-js-await-restricted-nested-after-4j8l.md => 1/1 files passed; cargo fmt --all; cargo test -p lyng-js-parser declarations -- --nocapture => 22 passed; cargo test -p lyng-js-parser modules -- --nocapture => 16 passed; git diff --check.

title: Fix staging public auto-accessor decorators
Fix staging public auto-accessor decorators

notes.new: Implemented public auto-accessor lowering for staging/decorators/public-auto-accessor.js. Reproduced before as 0/1 files, 2/2 variants failed with runtime TypeError in /tmp/lyng-js-decorators-public-auto-accessor-before.md. Root cause: parser represented public 'accessor x' as a plain public field, so class lowering installed data fields instead of prototype/static accessor descriptors. Fix threads a generated private backing name through ClassElement::Property, adds the backing field to class private layout, and emits synthetic getter/setter bytecode closures backed by private field get/set builtins. Added regression phase6_public_auto_accessors_install_backed_accessor_descriptors. title: Fix private field initialization before constructor defaults

notes.new: Implemented base class constructor instance-element initialization before parameter default evaluation. Reproduced staging/sm/PrivateName/constructor-args.js before as 0/1 files, 2/2 variants failed with runtime TypeError in /tmp/lyng-js-private-name-constructor-args-before.md. Root cause: FunctionCompiler::lower emitted class constructor field prologue after parameter binding/default initialization, so constructor(value = this.#x) read an uninitialized private field. Fix moves emit_class_constructor_field_prologue ahead of emit_parameter_environment_prologue; derived constructors are unchanged because the prologue already returns for derived plans and derived instance elements still run after super(). Added regression phase6_base_class_private_fields_initialize_before_constructor_defaults. title: Fix class strict execution contexts

notes.new: Implemented strict assignment lowering for class computed-name and heritage expression evaluation in sloppy outer code. Reproduced staging/sm/class/strictExecution.js before as 0/1 files failed, with only the non-strict variant failing Test262Error in /tmp/lyng-js-class-strict-execution-before.md. Root cause: class constructor bodies were already strict, but computed class element names were evaluated in the enclosing sloppy frame, so assignment to a non-extensible object silently failed instead of throwing TypeError. Fix adds StrictAssignNamedProperty and StrictAssignKeyedProperty bytecode opcodes, VM dispatch checks with a strict override, and a compiler force_strict_assignment mode used while lowering class heritage/computed key expressions. Added regression phase6_class_computed_names_use_strict_assignment_semantics. comments.author: 5341456+Feryla@users.noreply.github.com
comments.text: Partial implementation landed for direct-eval caller context propagation. Added direct-eval site flags for new.target/super allowances, carried those through direct-eval analysis and nested direct eval sites, preserved new.target on eval entry contexts, and made eval super-property contexts use lexical-this eval environments so outer method state is used. Added regression coverage for nested direct eval of new.target through ordinary functions/arrows while keeping top-level arrow eval invalid. Verification so far: cargo test -p lyng-js-tests direct_eval_propagates_new_target_context_to_nested_eval -- --nocapture; focused Test262 staging/sm/class/newTargetEval.js and staging/sm/class/superPropEvalInsideArrow.js both pass 2/2 variants; cargo fmt --all; git diff --check. Still in progress: derivedConstructorArrowEvalSuperCall.js now fails ReferenceError, and derivedConstructorArrowEvalNestedSuperCall.js still fails SyntaxError/derived super-call context; this appears tied to the broader arrow super() initialization path rather than the direct eval new.target/super-property path.

comments.author: 5341456+Feryla@users.noreply.github.com
comments.text: Implemented direct-eval propagation for new.target and super in class contexts. Direct eval sites now carry allow-new.target/allow-super flags through bytecode metadata and sema; dynamic eval analysis uses those flags plus caller function/home-object context to suppress only the allowed syntax diagnostics and to install eval entry state with the caller's new.target, home object, private env, and active function. Eval entry environments can now represent lexical this for carried active functions, super() lookup skips lexical arrow/eval function envs, and matching execution contexts sharing the lexical env get this-state updates after derived super calls. Added regressions for nested new.target eval and super() / super.property through caller arrows and eval-created arrows. Verified: cargo test -p lyng-js-tests direct_eval_preserves_super_context_through_arrows_and_nested_eval -- --nocapture; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests direct_eval_propagates_new_target_context_to_nested_eval -- --nocapture; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/newTargetEval.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-newTargetEval-final.md -j 1; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/superPropEvalInsideArrow.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-superPropEvalInsideArrow-final.md -j 1; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/derivedConstructorArrowEvalSuperCall.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-derivedConstructorArrowEvalSuperCall-final.md -j 1; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/derivedConstructorArrowEvalNestedSuperCall.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-derivedConstructorArrowEvalNestedSuperCall-final.md -j 1; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests eval -- --nocapture; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests classes -- --nocapture; cargo test -p lyng-js-compiler; cargo fmt --all; git diff --check.

Burn down Test262 staging failures and skips
description:
Classify and burn down the Test262 `staging/` category for Lyng JS, separating in-scope Stage 3+ ECMA-262 proposal behavior from SpiderMonkey-extension, host-only, or otherwise out-of-scope cases.

Current baseline from `reports/js/lyng-js/test262.md`: `staging` has 1484 selected files, 1477 runnable files, 1298 passed files, 179 failed files, 7 skipped files, and 0 panicked files. Variant totals show 2785 selected variants, 2771 runnable variants, 2462 passed variants, 309 failed variants, and 14 skipped variants. Visible clusters include `staging/sm/RegExp`, `staging/sm/Function`, strict-mode cases, classes/fields, Iterator helpers, TypedArray, RegExp named groups, explicit resource management, Temporal removed-method behavior, and some host-only `IsHTMLDDA` skips.

Relevant areas: `testdata/test262/test/staging/`, `reports/js/lyng-js/test262.md`, `reports/js/lyng-js/test262-exclusions.txt`, `tools/lyng-js-test262/`, and the parser/compiler/vm/builtins/runtime crates touched by each classified cluster.

Create focused child issues for the largest coherent clusters before implementation work so the staging tail does not become one unreviewable grab bag.
acceptance:
A staging classification pass identifies each remaining failure cluster as in-scope implementation work, harness/report work, host-only exclusion, proposal-stage exclusion, or out of scope.
In-scope staging failures are burned down with focused crate-level regression tests and targeted Test262 filtered runs.
A fresh `cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging --report /tmp/lyng-js-staging.md -j 4` run has 0 failed and 0 panicked tests for in-scope staging coverage.
Any remaining skipped or excluded staging tests are backed by explicit manifest entries and documented rationale, not implicit runner behavior.

Remaining clusters include explicit resource management, Array/TypedArray iterator/species/sort/timeouts, class eval super/new.target and related class semantics, eval global cases, object/accessor/proxy/Date/JSON/String mapping, and SpiderMonkey extension-only stress cases.

comments.author: 5341456+Feryla@users.noreply.github.com
comments.text: Reproduced before implementation: cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/superPropFor.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-superPropFor-before-423u.md -j 1 => 0/1 files passed, 2/2 variants failed with lowering error UnsupportedExpression. Root cause: for-in/of assignment heads use lower_assignment_target_from_register, whose member-expression branches treated super.prop/super[key] as ordinary member targets and tried to lower super as a value expression. The prepared reference path already handles super receiver/base/key correctly, but this assignment helper bypassed it. Added regression phase6_super_property_targets_work_in_for_in_and_for_of_heads covering super.prop in a for-in head and super["prop"] in a for-of head. Fix routes only super member assignment targets through prepare_reference_target(..., WriteOnly) and assign_prepared_reference, leaving ordinary member assignment lowering unchanged. Verification: cargo test -p lyng-js-tests phase6_super_property_targets_work_in_for_in_and_for_of_heads -- --nocapture; cargo run --release -p lyng-js-test262 -- --filter staging/sm/class/superPropFor.js --list-failures --report /tmp/lyng-js-class-superPropFor-after-423u.md -j 1; cargo test -p lyng-js-tests classes -- --nocapture; cargo test -p lyng-js-compiler; cargo fmt --all; git diff --check.
